next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The charting system at Lakeview was a hybrid — half-digital, half paper — that nobody had fully committed to in either direction. Carmen sat at the Wing A sub-station at 2:37 AM, entering vital signs from her 1 AM rounds into the computer while the paper backup waited on a clipboard beside her. The terminal was old enough that it hummed. The screen cast a blue-white wash over her hands and the laminate counter, which was peeling at the corner where someone had picked at it during hundreds of similar overnight hours.

She'd taken the double for the same reason she always did. The car insurance was due in nine days and she was $140 short. The overnight differential was $3.50 more per hour, which meant the sixteen extra hours bought her the gap plus gas money if she didn't eat out. She hadn't eaten since the turkey sandwich from the vending machine at 10 PM, and she could feel it — not hunger exactly, but a flatness in her concentration, a lag between noticing something on the screen and understanding what it said.

The building made its night sounds around her. The HVAC cycled on with a low shudder she felt in the counter surface before she heard it. Room 110 — Mrs. Okafor, ninety-one, end-stage COPD — produced the same thin, rhythmic moan she'd been producing for the seven months Carmen had worked this wing, a sound Carmen had stopped hearing the way you stop hearing a refrigerator. A toilet flushed somewhere in B Wing, probably Devon using the staff bathroom, which meant he was at least awake, which was an improvement over last Tuesday when she'd found him face...
open_hooks_to_carry: Harry gets out of his wheelchair and walks — the physical transformation
Ring meeting mechanics: Agnes sorting pills with pharmaceutical precision, Earl counting cash, Lola on lookout
prev_tail_excerpt:
...r, in patients who wandered at 3 AM and talked to people who weren't there and once, memorably, in Mr. Dietrich in 106 who'd gotten out of bed, put on his shoes, and tried to walk to a job he'd retired from in 1998.

That was sundowning. Sundowning was agitation and confusion and wandering. Sundowning was not three people sitting at a table having a structured conversation with papers and a pen and stopping in coordinated unison when they heard a footstep.

Carmen walked back to the nurse's station. Devon was asleep. She sat in the chair next to his and looked at the count log and the red folder with the discrepancy form that had her name and license number on it and the sticky note in her pocket with two numbers — three and five — and she added them together with what she'd just seen and the total didn't make sense in any clinical framework she owned.

Three oxycodone. Five alprazolam. Lola's four-second swallow. Agnes refusing for the first time in four months. Harry correcting her tramadol count two shifts ago with the precision of a man reading a number off a page. And now this: a room full of people who were supposed to be gone, sitting up in the dark, doing something that looked like work.

She didn't call anyone. She didn't write it down. She sat in the nurse's station and listened to Devon breathe and thought about what she'd seen and tried to build a version of events that didn't require the people she was paid to care for to be something other than what their charts said they were. She tried for a long time. The building hummed. The toilet ran. She couldn't do it.

setups_to_plant.description: Burt's Hungarian-English dictionary — a gift from his wife — is on his shelf and mentioned as meaningful to him; it is the pill stash's hiding place
Earl coughs during the meeting — a deep, wet rattle that he waves off; the first sign of his undiagnosed illness

state_deltas_required: the ring's operational demands and their awareness that Carmen is getting suspicious
the reader sees the full operation — its precision, its domestic absurdity, its human warmth — and learns Harry has identified Carmen as a threat
